volumeTitle: Birds. Lysistrata. Women at the Thesmophoria
label: Birds (Section 450 - Section 577)

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: σκοπεῖν δʼ ὅ τι ἂν προγράφωμεν ἐν τοῖς πινακίοις .
sections.translation: do not fail to read the decrees of dismissal we have posted .

sections.label: Section 1.451
sections.original: δολερὸν μὲν ἀεὶ κατὰ πάντα δὴ τρόπον
sections.translation: Man is a truly cunning creature , but nevertheless explain . Perhaps you are going to show me some good way

sections.label: Section 1.455
sections.original: δύναμίν τινα μείζω
sections.translation: to extend my power , some way that I have not had the wit to find out and which you have discovered . Speak ! ' tis to your own interest as well as to mine , for if you secure me some advantage , I will surely share it with you .

sections.label: Section 460
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἐφʼ ὅτῳπερ πράγματι τὴν σὴν ἥκεις γνώμην ἀναπείσας ,
sections.translation: But what object can have induced you to come among us ? Speak boldly , for I shall not break the truce , — until you have told us all .

sections.label: Section 462
sections.original: καὶ μὴν ὀργῶ νὴ τὸν Δία καὶ προπεφύραται λόγος εἷς μοι ,
sections.translation: I am bursting with desire to speak ; I have already mixed the dough of my address and nothing prevents me from kneading it .... Slave ! bring the chaplet and water , which you must pour over my hands . Be quick !

sections.label: Section 464b
sections.original: δειπνήσειν μέλλομεν ; ἢ τί ;
sections.translation: Is it a question of feasting ? What does it all mean ?

sections.label: Section 465
sections.original: μὰ Δίʼ ἀλλὰ λέγειν ζητῶ τι πάλαι μέγα καὶ λαρινὸν ἔπος τι ,
sections.translation: By Zeus , no ! but I am hunting for fine , tasty words to break down the hardness of their hearts . To the Chorus . I grieve so much for you , who at one time were kings ...

sections.label: Section 467b
sections.original: ἡμεῖς βασιλῆς ; τίνος ;
sections.translation: We kings ? Over whom ?

sections.label: Section 467c
sections.original: ὑμεῖς
sections.translation: ... of all that exists , firstly of me and of this man , even of Zeus himself . Your race is older than Saturn , the Titans and the Earth .

sections.label: Section 470b
sections.original: καὶ γῆς ;
sections.translation: What , older than the Earth !

sections.label: Section 470c
sections.original: νὴ τὸν Ἀπόλλω .
sections.translation: By Phoebus , yes .

sections.label: Section 470d
sections.original: τουτὶ μὰ Δίʼ οὐκ ἐπεπύσμην .
sections.translation: By Zeus , but I never knew that before !

sections.label: Section 471
sections.original: ἀμαθὴς γὰρ ἔφυς κοὐ πολυπράγμων , οὐδʼ Αἴσωπον πεπάτηκας ,
sections.translation: That ' s because you are ignorant and heedless , and have never read your Aesop . He is the one who tells us that the lark was born before all other creatures , indeed before the Earth ; his father died of sickness , but the Earth did not exist then ; he remained unburied for five days , when the bird in its dilemma decided ,

sections.label: Section 475
sections.original: ὑπʼ ἀμηχανίας τὸν πατέρʼ αὑτῆς ἐν τῇ κεφαλῇ κατορύξαι .
sections.translation: for want of a better place , to entomb its father in its own head .

sections.label: Section 476
sections.original: ὁ πατὴρ ἄρα τῆς κορυδοῦ νυνὶ κεῖται τεθνεὼς Κεφαλῆσιν .
sections.translation: So that the lark ' s father is buried at Cephalae .

sections.label: Section 477
sections.original: οὔκουν δῆτʼ εἰ πρότεροι μὲν γῆς πρότεροι δὲ θεῶν ἐγένοντο ,
sections.translation: Hence , if they existed before the Earth , before the gods , the kingship belongs to them by right of priority .

sections.label: Section 479
sections.original: νὴ τὸν Ἀπόλλω · πάνυ τοίνυν χρὴ ῥύγχος βόσκειν σε τὸ λοιπόν ·
sections.translation: Undoubtedly , but sharpen your beak well ;

sections.label: Section 480
sections.original: οὐκ ἀποδώσει ταχέως ὁ Ζεὺς τὸ σκῆπτρον τῷ δρυκολάπτῃ .
sections.translation: Zeus won ' t be in a hurry to hand over his scepter to the woodpecker .

sections.label: Section 481
sections.original: ὡς δʼ οὐχὶ θεοὶ τοίνυν ἦρχον τῶν ἀνθρώπων τὸ παλαιόν ,
sections.translation: It was not the gods , but the birds , who were formerly the masters and kings over men ; of this I have a thousand proofs . First of all , I will point you to the cock , who governed the Persians before all other monarchs , before Darius and Megabazus .

sections.label: Section 485
sections.original: ὥστε καλεῖται Περσικὸς ὄρνις ἀπὸ τῆς ἀρχῆς ἔτʼ ἐκείνης .
sections.translation: It ' s in memory of his reign that he is called the Persian bird .

sections.label: Section 486
sections.original: διὰ ταῦτʼ ἄρʼ ἔχων καὶ νῦν ὥσπερ βασιλεὺς ὁ μέγας διαβάσκει
sections.translation: For this reason also , even to-day , he alone of all the birds wears his tiara straight on his head , like the Great King .

sections.label: Section 488
sections.original: οὕτω δʼ ἴσχυσέ τε καὶ μέγας ἦν τότε καὶ πολύς , ὥστʼ ἔτι καὶ νῦν
sections.translation: He was so strong , so great , so feared , that even now , on account of his ancient power ,

sections.label: Section 490
sections.original: ἀναπηδῶσιν πάντες ἐπʼ ἔργον χαλκῆς κεραμῆς σκυλοδέψαι
sections.translation: everyone jumps out of bed as soon as ever he crows at daybreak . Blacksmiths , potters , tanners , shoemakers , bathmen , corn-dealers , lyre-makers and armorers , all put on their shoes and go to work before it is daylight .

sections.label: Section 492b
sections.original: ἐμὲ τοῦτό γʼ ἐρώτα .
sections.translation: I can tell you something about that . It was the cock ' s fault that I lost a splendid tunic of Phrygian wool . I was at a feast in town , given to celebrate the birth of a child ; I had drunk pretty freely

sections.label: Section 495
sections.original: κἄρτι καθηῦδον , καὶ πρὶν δειπνεῖν τοὺς ἄλλους οὗτος ἄρʼ ᾖσεν ·
sections.translation: and had just fallen asleep , when a cock , I suppose in a greater hurry than the rest , began to crow . I thought it was dawn and set out for Halimus . I had hardly got beyond the walls , when a footpad struck me in the back with his bludgeon ; down I went and wanted to shout , but he had already made off with my mantle .

sections.label: Section 499
sections.original: ἰκτῖνος δʼ οὖν τῶν Ἑλλήνων ἦρχεν τότε κἀβασίλευεν .
sections.translation: Formerly also the kite was ruler and king over the Greeks .

sections.label: Section 500
sections.original: τῶν Ἑλλήνων ;
sections.translation: The Greeks ?

sections.label: Section 500b
sections.original: καὶ κατέδειξέν γʼ οὗτος πρῶτος βασιλεύων
sections.translation: And when he was king , he was the one who first taught them to fall on their knees before the kites .

sections.label: Section 501b
sections.original: νὴ τὸν Διόνυσον , ἐγὼ γοῦν
sections.translation: By Zeus ! that ' s what I did myself one day on seeing a kite ; but at the moment I was on my knees , and leaning backwards with mouth agape , I bolted an obolus and was forced to carry my meal-sack home empty .

sections.label: Section 504
sections.original: Αἰγύπτου δʼ αὖ καὶ Φοινίκης πάσης κόκκυξ βασιλεὺς ἦν ·
sections.translation: The cuckoo was king of Egypt and of the whole of Phoenicia .

sections.label: Section 505
sections.original: χὠπόθʼ ὁ κόκκυξ εἴποι κόκκυ , τότʼ ἂν οἱ Φοίνικες ἅπαντες
sections.translation: When he called out cuckoo , all the Phoenicians hurried to the fields to reap their wheat and their barley .

sections.label: Section 507
sections.original: τοῦτʼ ἄρʼ ἐκεῖνʼ ἦν τοὔπος ἀληθῶς · κόκκυψωλοὶ πεδίονδε .
sections.translation: Hence no doubt the proverb , Cuckoo ! cuckoo ! go to the fields , ye circumcised .

sections.label: Section 508
sections.original: ἦρχον δʼ οὕτω σφόδρα τὴν ἀρχήν , ὥστʼ εἴ τις καὶ βασιλεύοι
sections.translation: So powerful were the birds that the kings of Grecian cities , Agamemnon , Menelaus , for instance ,

sections.label: Section 510
sections.original: ἐπὶ τῶν σκήπτρων ἐκάθητʼ ὄρνις μετέχων ὅ τι δωροδοκοίη .
sections.translation: carried a bird on the tip of their scepters , who had his share of all presents .

sections.label: Section 511
sections.original: τουτὶ τοίνυν οὐκ ᾔδη ʼγώ · καὶ δῆτά μʼ ἐλάμβανε θαῦμα ,
sections.translation: That I didn ' t know and was much astonished when I saw Priam come upon the stage in the tragedies with a bird , which kept watching Lysicrates to see if he got any present .

sections.label: Section 514
sections.original: ὃ δὲ δεινότατόν γʼ ἐστὶν ἁπάντων , ὁ Ζεὺς γὰρ ὁ νῦν βασιλεύων
sections.translation: But the strongest proof of all is that Zeus , who now reigns ,

sections.label: Section 515
sections.original: αἰετὸν ὄρνιν ἕστηκεν ἔχων ἐπὶ τῆς κεφαλῆς βασιλεὺς ὤν ,
sections.translation: is represented as standing with an eagle on his head as a symbol of his royalty ; his daughter has an owl , and Phoebus , as his servant , has a hawk .

sections.label: Section 517
sections.original: νὴ τὴν Δήμητρʼ εὖ ταῦτα λέγεις . τίνος οὕνεκα ταῦτʼ ἄρʼ ἔχουσιν ;
sections.translation: By Demeter , the point is well taken . But what are all these birds doing in heaven ?

sections.label: Section 518
sections.original: ἵνʼ ὅταν θύων τις ἔπειτʼ αὐτοῖς ἐς τὴν χεῖρʼ , ὡς νόμος ἐστίν ,
sections.translation: When anyone sacrifices and , according to the rite , offers the entrails to the gods , these birds take their share before Zeus .

sections.label: Section 520
sections.original: ὤμνυ τʼ οὐδεὶς τότʼ ἂν ἀνθρώπων θεόν , ἀλλʼ ὄρνιθας ἅπαντες ·
sections.translation: Formerly men always swore by the birds and never by the gods . And even now Lampon swears by the goose whenever he wishes to deceive someone . Thus it is clear that you were once great and sacred ,

sections.label: Section 523
sections.original: νῦν δʼ ἀνδράποδʼ ἠλιθίους Μανᾶς ·
sections.translation: but now you are looked upon as slaves , as fools , as Maneses ; stones

sections.label: Section 525
sections.original: βάλλουσʼ ὑμᾶς , κἀν τοῖς ἱεροῖς
sections.translation: are thrown at you as at raving madmen , even in holy places . A crowd of bird-catchers sets snares , traps , limed twigs and nets of all sorts for you ; you are caught , you are sold in heaps

sections.label: Section 530
sections.original: οἱ δʼ ὠνοῦνται βλιμάζοντες ·
sections.translation: and the buyers finger you over to be certain you are fat . Again , if they would but serve you up simply roasted ; but they rasp cheese into a mixture of oil , vinegar and laserwort ,

sections.label: Section 535
sections.original: κατάχυσμʼ ἕτερον γλυκὺ καὶ λιπαρόν ,
sections.translation: to which another sweet and greasy sauce is added , and the whole is poured scalding hot over your back , for all the world as if you were diseased meat .

sections.label: Section 539
sections.original: πολὺ δὴ πολὺ δὴ χαλεπωτάτους λόγους
sections.translation: Man , your words have

sections.label: Section 540
sections.original: ἤνεγκας ἄνθρωφʼ . ὡς ἐδάκρυσά γʼ ἐμῶν
sections.translation: made my heart bleed ; I have groaned over the treachery of our fathers , who knew not how to transmit to us the high rank they held from their forefathers . But ' tis a benevolent Genius , a happy Fate , that sends you to us ;

sections.label: Section 545
sections.original: ἀγαθὴν ἥκεις ἐμοὶ σωτήρ .
sections.translation: you shall be our deliverer and I place the destiny of my little ones and my own in your hands with every confidence .

sections.label: Section 548
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ὅ τι χρὴ δρᾶν , σὺ δίδασκε παρών · ὡς ζῆν οὐκ ἄξιον ἡμῖν ,
sections.translation: But hasten to tell me what must be done ; we should not be worthy to live , if we did not seek to regain our royalty by every possible means .

sections.label: Section 550
sections.original: καὶ δὴ τοίνυν πρῶτα διδάσκω μίαν ὀρνίθων πόλιν εἶναι ,
sections.translation: First I advise that the birds gather together in one city and that they build a wall of great bricks , like that at Babylon , round the plains of the air and the whole region of space that divides earth from heaven .

sections.label: Section 553
sections.original: ὦ Κεβριόνη καὶ Πορφυρίων ὡς σμερδαλέον τὸ πόλισμα .
sections.translation: Oh , Cebriones ! oh , Porphyrion ! what a terribly strong place !

sections.label: Section 554
sections.original: κἄπειτʼ ἢν τοῦτʼ ἐπανεστήκῃ , τὴν ἀρχὴν τὸν Δίʼ ἀπαιτεῖν ·
sections.translation: Then , when this has been well done and completed , you demand back the empire from Zeus ;

sections.label: Section 555
sections.original: κἂν μὲν μὴ φῇ μηδʼ ἐθελήσῃ μηδʼ εὐθὺς γνωσιμαχήσῃ ,
sections.translation: if he will not agree , if he refuses and does not at once confess himself beaten , you declare a sacred war against him and forbid the gods henceforward to pass through your country with their tools up , as hitherto , for the purpose of laying their Alcmenas , their Alopes , or their Semeles ! if they try to pass through ,

sections.label: Section 560
sections.original: σφραγῖδʼ αὐτοῖς ἐπὶ τὴν ψωλήν , ἵνα μὴ βινῶσʼ ἔτʼ ἐκείνας .
sections.translation: you put rings on their tools so that they can ' t make love any longer . You send another messenger to mankind , who will proclaim to them that the birds are kings , that for the future they must first of all sacrifice to them , and only afterwards to the gods ; that it is fitting to appoint to each deity the bird that has most in common with it .

sections.label: Section 565
sections.original: ἢν Ἀφροδίτῃ θύῃ , πυροὺς ὄρνιθι φαληρίδι θύειν ·
sections.translation: For instance , are they sacrificing to Aphrodite , let them at the same time offer barley to the coot ; are they immolating a sheep to Poseidon , let them consecrate wheat in honor of the duck ; if a steer is being offered to Heracles , let honey-cakes be dedicated to the gull ; if a goat is being slain for King Zeus , there is a King-Bird , the wren , to whom the sacrifice of a male gnat is due before Zeus himself even .

sections.label: Section 570
sections.original: ἥσθην σέρφῳ σφαγιαζομένῳ . βροντάτω νῦν ὁ μέγας Ζάν .
sections.translation: This notion of an immolated gnat delights me ! And now let the great Zeus thunder !

sections.label: Section 571
sections.original: καὶ πῶς ἡμᾶς νομιοῦσι θεοὺς ἄνθρωποι κοὐχὶ κολοιούς ,
sections.translation: But how will mankind recognize us as gods and not as jays ? Us , who have wings and fly ?

sections.label: Section 572b
sections.original: ληρεῖς · καὶ νὴ Δίʼ ὅ γʼ Ἑρμῆς
sections.translation: You talk rubbish ! Hermes is a god and has wings and flies , and so do many other gods . First of all , Victory flies with golden wings , Eros is undoubtedly winged too ,

sections.label: Section 575
sections.original: Ἥρην δέ γʼ Ὅμηρος ἔφασκʼ ἰκέλην εἶναι τρήρωνι πελείῃ .
sections.translation: and Iris is compared by Homer to a timorous dove .

sections.label: Section 576
sections.original: ὁ Ζεὺς δʼ ἡμῖν οὐ βροντήσας πέμψει πτερόεντα κεραυνόν ;
sections.translation: But will not Zeus thunder and send his winged bolts against us ?

sections.label: Section 577
sections.original: ἢν δʼ οὖν ὑμᾶς μὲν ὑπʼ ἀγνοίας εἶναι νομίσωσι τὸ μηδέν ,
sections.translation: If men in their blindness do not recognize us as gods and so continue to worship the dwellers in Olympus ? Then a cloud of sparrows greedy for corn must descend upon their fields and eat up all their seeds ;
